---
title: "Experimenting with coring and subcoring in Bourgneuf Bay"
author: "Lee, Chengfa Benjamin"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: bookdown::html_document2
bibliography: references.bib 
editor_options:
  chunk_output_type: inline
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)

library(magrittr)
library(sf)
library(leaflet)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(stringr)
library(ggplot2)
# library(ggiraph)
# library(ggpubr)
# library(patchwork)
```

This page explores the use of a [Russian Peat Corer](https://www.vanwalt.com/news/2014/05/15/peat-sampler-or-russian-corer-either-way-a-really-useful-sediment-sampling-tool/) (Figure \@ref(fig:russianCorer)). 

```{r russianCorer}
#| echo: false
#| error: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| out.width: "49%"
#| fig.align: 'center'
#| fig.show: 'hold'
#| fig.cap: 'Russian peat corer. It has a sampling length of 50 cm and a spearhead. The corer is first pushed upright and spearheaded down into the ground. Once the correct depth is reached, the user twists the handle in a clockwise direction to gouge the substrate sample into the corer and simultaneously close the metal flap over the sampled soil. The corer can then be safely extracted from the ground to collect the cored substrate.'

knitr::include_graphics('img/russian_corer_standing.jpg')
# knitr::include_graphics(c('img/russian_corer_standing.jpg','img/russian_corer_lying.jpg'))
```

Subcoring or slicing the core is a common scientific approach as it gives a detailed vertical profile of the seabed substrate. In exchange, subcoring increases the required laboratory work in proportion to the number of the subcores/slices made. Furthermore, as part of a larger research goal to map the seagrass carbon in Europe, the cores are used in conjunction with optical remote sensing. The surficial nature of optical remote sensing renders the vertical profile moot, as the detailed benthic substrate information cannot be easily derived from optical remote sensing. Nonetheless, a simplified representation of the benthic substrate information could still be possible by proxy(ies). One possibility is to sample the core as a whole rather than as subcores. 

This study inspects the difference between using the whole core and the aggregated results of the subcores as a representation for optical remote sensing. 

All analyses were performed in R version 4.5.2 [@Rversion].
All work belong to the [REWRITE project](https://rewriteproject.eu/).

# Method

## Sample collection

The fieldwork was conducted in Bourgneuf Bay. Three replicates each were collected in the persistent seagrass meadow and a sandier area. All GPS positions were collected using the SW Maps version 3.0.14.3 [@SWMapsversion] on a Google Pixel 9.

```{r samplingDesign}
#| echo: false
#| error: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

stations <- read_sf('data/shp/photos.shp') %>%
  select(!PATH) %>%
  filter(!REMARKS %in% c('Mud_1', 'non-stable site'))

map_centre <- stations %>% 
  st_union() %>%
  st_centroid() %>%
  st_coordinates()

pal.shp <- colorFactor(c('navy','yellow3'), domain = c('mix','slice'))

m.shp <- leaflet(stations) %>%
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.PositronNoLabels) %>%
  setView(map_centre[1], map_centre[2], 15) %>%
  addCircleMarkers(radius = 2, color = ~pal.shp(Method), fillOpacity = 1, label = ~REMARKS) %>%
  addLegend(pal = pal.shp, values = ~Method, opacity = 1)

m.shp
```

For each replicate, a 1-metre whole core and 1-metre subcoring core was collected. As the Russian peat corer has a sampling length of 50 cm, the 1-metre core was further split into two 50 cm samples, one at the 0-50 cm depth and the other at the 50-100 cm depth. Due to the spearhead design of the Russian peat corer, the 50-100 cm depth was sampled next to the 0-50 cm depth, rather than immediately below since the spearhead would have disrupted the area below during the coring process (Figures \@ref(fig:coringProcessMud) and \@ref(fig:coringProcessSand)). The subcoring core was sliced in the field at 10 cm per subcore and separated into different collection bags. Meanwhile, for the whole core approach, both top and bottom cores were mixed in a bucket and then transferred into a single, large collection bag. For the first and the second replicate in the sand area, the corer for the subcore could only go up to a depth of about 75 cm and 80 cm, respectively. As such, they were sampled to 70 cm and 80 cm, respectively, for both the subcore and whole core approaches to ensure consistency within a replicate (Figure \@ref(fig:coringProcessSand)).

The samples were then transported to back to the laboratory and preserved in a 4 &deg;C fridge.

```{r coringProcessPreprocessing}
#| echo: false
#| error: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

dir.photos <- list.files('data/photos') %>%
  paste0('data/photos/', .)
```

```{r coringProcessMud}
#| echo: false
#| error: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| out.width: "24.9%"
#| fig.align: 'center'
#| fig.show: 'hold'
#| fig.cap: 'Sampled cores at the persistent seagrass meadow. Each row shows a set of replicate. The first two photographs on the left depicts the 0-50 cm and 50-100 cm cores for the subcore approach. The last two photographs on the right depicts the 0-50 m and 50-100 cm cores for the whole core approach.'

dir.photos <- list.files('data/photos') %>%
  grep('Mud', ., value = TRUE) %>%
  paste0('data/photos/', .) %>%
  "["(c(4:1,8:5,12:9))

knitr::include_graphics(dir.photos)
```

```{r coringProcessSand}
#| echo: false
#| error: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| out.width: "24.9%"
#| fig.align: 'center'
#| fig.show: 'hold'
#| fig.cap: 'Sampled cores at the sandier area. Each row shows a set of replicate. The first two photographs on the left depicts the 0-50 cm and 50-100 cm cores for the subcore approach. The last two photographs on the right depicts the 0-50 m and 50-100 cm cores for the whole core approach. For the first and the second replicate, the corer for the subcore could only go up to a depth of about 75 cm and 80 cm, respectively. As such, they were sampled to 70 cm and 80 cm, respectively, for both the subcore and whole core approaches to ensure consistency within a replicate.'

dir.photos <- list.files('data/photos') %>%
  grep('Sand', ., value = TRUE) %>%
  paste0('data/photos/', .) %>%
  "["(c(4:1,8:5,12:9))

knitr::include_graphics(dir.photos)
```


## Sample drying and weighing

Using a syringe, 20 ml of samples were obtained from each sample bag. The extracted samples were weighed before and after they were dried. For the drying process, the extracted samples were dried in an oven at 60 &deg;C for 72 hours and left to cool in a dessicator for at least one hour . Based on equation \@ref(eq:dryBulkDensity), the dry bulk density was then calculated using the weight of the dried soil and the volume extracted by the syringe [@theBlueCarbonInitiative].

\begin{equation}
Dry\,bulk\,density\,(g/cm^3) = \frac{Mass\,of\,dry\,soil\,(g)}{Orignal\,volume\,sampled\,(cm^3)} (\#eq:dryBulkDensity)
\end{equation}


## Granulometry

This section will be updated when the work is completed.


## TOC measurement

This section will be updated when the work is completed.


## Spatial upscaling

This section will be updated when the mapping work is completed.


## Uncertainty

This section will be updated when the work is completed.


# Results

## Dry Bulk Density

```{r dryBulkDensityPreprocessing}
#| echo: false
#| error: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

csv.dbd <- read.csv('data/csv/Bourgneuf_bulkdensity.csv', sep = ';') %>%
  filter(!is.na(slice_id)) %>%
  select(!c(Photo_ID, REC_TIME, LATITUDE, LONGITUDE))

## Although there are two entries for the whole core approach (top 0-50 cm and bottom 50-100 cm cores), 
## the distinction is less important here as there is no spatial analysis.
## Thus, the "repeat row" of the bottom core entry is removed.
csv.dbd <- csv.dbd %>%
  filter(!is.na(dry_weight))

## Calculations
csv.dbd <- csv.dbd %>%
  mutate(rep_id = paste(ID, Method, sep = "_"),
         # actual weight without bottle
         soil_wet_weight = wet_weight - bottle_weight,
         soil_dry_weight = dry_weight - bottle_weight,
         # dry bulk density
         dry_bulk_density = soil_dry_weight / 20
         ) %>%
  separate(ID, c(NA, 'rep'), remove = FALSE)

csv.dbd.average <- csv.dbd %>%
  # summarise data
  group_by(ID, rep_id, Method, substrate) %>%
  summarise(mean_soil_wet_weight = mean(soil_wet_weight), mean_soil_dry_weight = mean(soil_dry_weight), mean_dry_bulk_density = mean(dry_bulk_density), syringe_diameter = mean(syringe_diameter)) %>%
  ungroup() %>% 
  # calculate differences between slice & mix
  group_by(ID) %>%
  mutate(# calculate absolute differences
    diff_mean_soil_wet_weight = diff(mean_soil_wet_weight), 
    diff_mean_soil_dry_weight = diff(mean_soil_dry_weight), 
    diff_mean_dry_bulk_density = diff(mean_dry_bulk_density),
    # calculate percentage differences (remove signs)
    percent_diff_soil_wet_weight = abs(diff_mean_soil_wet_weight/mean_soil_wet_weight)*100, 
    percent_diff_soil_dry_weight = abs(diff_mean_soil_dry_weight/mean_soil_dry_weight)*100, 
    percent_diff_dry_bulk_density = abs(diff_mean_dry_bulk_density/mean_dry_bulk_density)*100
    ) %>%
  ungroup()


csv.dbd <- csv.dbd %>%
  filter(Method == 'mix') %>%
  mutate(slice_start = slice_end - 10) %>%
  rbind(csv.dbd) 

csv.dbd <- {
  # add a dupe row (repeat row had NA in all values) with a different slice start for the purpose of plotting
  t <- csv.dbd %>% 
    filter(Method == 'mix') %>%
    select(-c(Method, soil_wet_weight, soil_dry_weight, dry_bulk_density))
  
  # include the averaged slice data for the purpose of plotting
  m <- csv.dbd.average %>%
    filter(Method == 'slice') %>%
    select(ID, mean_soil_wet_weight, mean_soil_dry_weight, mean_dry_bulk_density) %>%
    rename_with(.fn = ~ gsub("mean_", "", .x), .cols = starts_with('mean_'))
  
  j <- right_join(t, m, by = join_by(ID), relationship = 'many-to-one') %>%
    mutate(Method = 'slice_averaged')
  
  rbind(csv.dbd, j)
}

# label cleaning
csv.dbd$Method <- factor(csv.dbd$Method, levels = c('mix', 'slice', 'slice_averaged'), labels = c('Whole core (mixed)', 'Subcoring', 'Subcoring (averaged)'))
csv.dbd$substrate <- str_to_title(csv.dbd$substrate)
```

The difference in dry bulk density between the mixed whole core and the averaged slices ranged between `r csv.dbd.average %>% filter(substrate == 'mud') %>% select(percent_diff_dry_bulk_density) %>% min() %>% format(digits = 2, nsmall = 1)`% and `r csv.dbd.average %>% filter(substrate == 'mud') %>% select(percent_diff_dry_bulk_density) %>% max() %>% format(digits = 2, nsmall = 1)`% for the persistent seagrass meadow with muddy substrate and `r csv.dbd.average %>% filter(substrate == 'sand') %>% select(percent_diff_dry_bulk_density) %>% min() %>% format(digits = 2, nsmall = 1)`% and `r csv.dbd.average %>% filter(substrate == 'sand') %>% select(percent_diff_dry_bulk_density) %>% max() %>% format(digits = 2, nsmall = 1)`% for the sandy substrate. Notably, there was a huge difference in the 'Sand 3' replicate (Figure \@ref(fig:dryBulkDensityVerticalProfile)). When 'Sand 3' is excluded, the largest difference is `r csv.dbd.average %>% filter(!ID == 'Sand_3') %>% select(percent_diff_dry_bulk_density) %>% max() %>% format(digits = 2, nsmall = 1)`%. The presence of shells in the slices did not cause any seemingly anomalous patterns to the vertical profile.

```{r dryBulkDensityVerticalProfile}
#| echo: false
#| error: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig.width: 8
#| fig.height: 6
#| fig.align: 'center'
#| fig.cap: Benthic soil/substrate dry bulk density vertical profile at Bourgneuf Bay. The two different methods are represented by different colours and the averaged values of all the slices are represented by a dotted line. Any points with the presence of shells in the sample are highlighted with a solid dot.

p.dbd.vertprof <- csv.dbd %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = slice_start + 5, y = dry_bulk_density, colour = Method)) +
  geom_line(aes(linetype = Method), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(shape = shell), size = 2.5) +
  xlim(c(100,0)) + ylim(c(0, max(csv.dbd$dry_bulk_density))) +coord_flip() +
  xlab('Depth (cm)') + ylab(bquote('Dry Bulk Density'~(g/cm^3))) +
  scale_colour_manual('Method', values = c('navy', 'yellow3', 'orange')) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c(1, 1, 2)) +
  scale_shape_manual('Presence\nof shell', values = c(NA, 19)) +
  facet_grid(substrate ~ rep) +
  ggtitle('Vertical profile of the benthic dry bulk density')

p.dbd.vertprof
```

## TOC measurement


## Spatial upscaling

This section will be updated when the mapping work is completed.

# References
